title: The Weak Link
passage:
When the logistics researcher Priya Valk fitted temperature loggers to a fleet of forty refrigerated lorries, she assumed the long motorway hauls would be where the cold chain failed. They were not. Across a full winter of deliveries, her loggers showed that the reefer units held their set point steadily for hour after hour on the road; the sharp, damaging spikes came instead during the brief handovers, when pallets stood on unheated — but far from cold — loading docks while paperwork was checked and rear doors hung open. Valk is cautious about how far the finding travels. "We measured one operator, over one winter," she notes; a warmer region, or a fleet with tighter dock discipline, might shift the picture entirely. Still, she suspects the weak link in the chain is not the journey but the pause.
— Rowan Iversen, trade desk

questions.prompt: According to the text, what did Valk's loggers show about where the cold chain broke down?

options.text: The reefer units failed to hold their set point across the long motorway hauls, and that was where most of the spoilage began.
Opening the lorries' rear doors while they were driving on the motorway was the main cause of the temperature spikes.
Temperatures held steady during the long road hauls, and the damaging spikes came instead during the brief handovers on the loading docks.
Warm loading docks were shown to ruin the great majority of every operator's deliveries throughout the region across the year.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), short text. STEM-LAW: the stem asks where the chain broke down but does not entail the road-versus-dock direction. WHY-KEY (C): the passage states 'the reefer units held their set point steadily for hour after hour on the road; the sharp, damaging spikes came instead during the brief handovers, when pallets stood on unheated ... loading docks'. C paraphrases that road-steady / dock-spike contrast. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = reversal / contradiction — inverts the finding, putting the failure on the motorway hauls the text says held steady; tempting because it matches Valk's own initial assumption, which the text records and then overturns ('They were not'). B = wrong_location / outside_knowledge — the open rear doors belong to the dock handovers, not to motorway driving, so the distractor relocates a real detail to the wrong phase; tempting because doors and spikes are both genuinely in the text. D = quantifier_upgrade / contradiction — 'warm' contradicts the docks being 'unheated — but far from cold', and 'great majority of every operator's deliveries throughout the region' overstates a study Valk explicitly limits to 'one operator, over one winter'; tempting because it sounds like a bold headline version of the result.

generator_meta.topic: cold-chain food logistics field study: temperature loggers showing dock-handover spikes, not motorway hauls, as the weak link in refrigerated transport

fictional_source.byline: Rowan Iversen, invented trade journalist; research attributed to invented researcher Priya Valk

fictional_entities: Priya Valk (invented researcher; all findings invented)
generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout ('lorries', 'motorway', 'favoured'-family none present); no AmE-only spellings.
generator_meta.engineered_traps: reversal at A (matches Valk's overturned assumption), wrong_location at B (dock doors relocated to motorway), quantifier_upgrade + 'warm' contradiction at D; key C is the confident specific paraphrase, and the absolute distractor (D) also carries an independent content flaw ('warm' vs 'far from cold'), so it is not eliminable by form alone.
